summary: Soviet film director and film theorist, pioneer of montage theory. Best known for Battleship Potemkin (1925), featuring the 'Odessa Steps' sequence, and Alexander Nevsky (1938). Also directed Strike (1925), October (1928), and the unfinished ¡Que Viva México! (1932). Developed five types of montage and argued that 'montage is conflict.' Associated with Soviet cinema of the 1920s–1940s.

works.name: Battleship Potemkin (1925)
works.description: Silent film depicting the 1905 Potemkin mutiny. Most famous for the 'Odessa Steps' sequence, in which a baby carriage rolls down the steps as soldiers fire on civilians. Widely considered a landmark of montage technique. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Baby carriage rolls down the Odessa Steps as soldiers/militia fire on civilians
examples: A baby carriage rolls down the Odessa Steps in a scene from this director's film Battleship Potemkin
In a film by this director, a baby in a carriage is let loose during the Odessa Steps sequence

clues.clue: Eisenstein edited the 'Odessa Steps' montage — giveaway identifier for Eisenstein
examples: what theorist edited the 'Odessa steps' montage in his film Battleship Potemkin?

works.name: Strike (1925)
works.description: Eisenstein's debut feature. The climactic scene intercuts graphic footage of cows being slaughtered with soldiers massacring workers in a muddy field. An early showcase of his montage theory. Eisenstein's first film was the short Glumov's Diary. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Climax intercuts footage of cows being slaughtered with soldiers murdering laborers in a muddy field
examples: shots of cows being slaughtered are intercut with a militia murdering laborers in a field in the climactic scene of the 1925 film Strike
this director cut between graphic footage of cows being slaughtered with soldiers firing on crowds of people in a muddy field

clues.clue: Eisenstein's first feature film; his very first film was Glumov's Diary
examples: This director's first film, Glumov's Diary, uses several visual gags to portray the book's contents

works.name: Alexander Nevsky (1938)
works.description: Propaganda film dramatizing the Battle of the Ice (Battle on the Ice, 1242), in which Russian prince Alexander Nevsky defeats the Teutonic Knights. A bishop of the Teutonic Order wears a hat with a swastika. Score by Sergei Prokofiev. Restored Eisenstein's political reputation after October. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Dramatizes the Battle of the Ice; a bishop of the Teutonic Order wears a hat with a swastika
examples: A bishop of the Teutonic Order wears a hat with a swastika in a film by this director that restored his political reputation by dramatizing the Battle of the Ice

clues.clue: Made as a propaganda film in the face of German expansionist rhetoric; restored Eisenstein's political reputation
examples: Alexander Nevsky, a film by this director, was produced as a propaganda feature in the face of German expansionist rhetoric

works.name: October / Ten Days That Shook the World (1928)
works.description: Silent film about the 1917 Russian Revolution. The arts journal October (founded by Rosalind Krauss and Annette Michelson) is named after this film. Eisenstein was later accused of 'formalism' for making it. Ends with a shot of a ruler's bearded head towering over a parade demanding his return. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Ends with a shot of a ruler's bearded head appearing to tower over a parade demanding his return
examples: this director created a shot in which a ruler's bearded head appears to tower over a parade demanding his return in the background

clues.clue: Inspired the name of the arts journal October, founded by Rosalind Krauss and Annette Michelson
examples: This theorist directed a film that inspired the name of an arts journal founded by Rosalind Krauss and Annette Michelson

clues.clue: Soviet leaders accused Eisenstein of 'formalism' for this film about the 1917 Revolution
examples: Leaders in this country charged Eisenstein with being a 'formalist' after he made October about the 1917 Revolution

works.name: ¡Que Viva México! (unfinished, 1932)
works.description: Unfinished epic about the entire history of Mexico. Eisenstein shot 50 hours of footage but never finished it. Upton Sinclair (at Chaplin's request) financed the project. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Abandoned after 50 hours of footage; Upton Sinclair financed it (at Chaplin's request)
examples: This director abandoned ¡Que viva Mexico!, his attempt to capture the entire history of Mexico on film, after shooting 50 hours of footage and failing to come up with an idea for an actual movie
Charlie Chaplin helped a director from this country meet Upton and Mary Sinclair to make the unfinished epic film ¡Que viva Mexico!

works.name: Ivan the Terrible (1944–1946)
works.description: Planned trilogy about Tsar Ivan IV. Part II was condemned by the Soviet Central Committee as 'anti-historical'; Eisenstein died of a heart attack in 1948 after refusing to revise it. Wikipedia

clues.clue: In 1946, Eisenstein died of a heart attack after the Central Committee condemned Part II of this trilogy as 'anti-historical' and he chose not to revise it
examples: In 1946, this director died of a heart attack after he chose not to revise the second part of a trilogy deemed 'anti-historical' by the Central Committee

works.name: Montage Theory
works.description: Eisenstein's central theoretical contribution to cinema — the idea that the collision of two shots creates a new meaning beyond either alone. He identified five types: metric, rhythmic, tonal, overtonal, and intellectual. Key essays collected in Film Form (1949). He collaborated with Vsevolod Pudovkin on montage theory. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Developed five types of montage — metric, rhythmic, tonal, overtonal, intellectual — with peers like Vsevolod Pudovkin
examples: This theorist used the terms 'intellectual,' 'overtonal,' 'tonal,' 'rhythmic,' and 'metric' to describe the five types of a concept he developed with peers like Vsevolod Pudovkin

clues.clue: Film Form essay collection; argued 'montage is conflict'; studied the emotions induced by haiku and kabuki
examples: In their collection of essays Film Form, this theorist stated that 'montage is conflict'
In an essay on cinema's 'fourth dimension,' this theorist analyzed the emotions induced by haiku and kabuki

clues.clue: Essay argued Charles Dickens was the strongest influence on D.W. Griffith
examples: This theorist remarked that 'movies were boiling' in the kettle described in the opening line of The Cricket in the Hearth, in an essay positing that Charles Dickens was the strongest influence on D.W. Griffith

clues.clue: In a lecture, insisted Paul Robeson should wield a candelabrum to star in a film about Jean-Jacques Dessalines
examples: In a lecture series, this theorist insisted that Paul Robeson should wield a candelabrum to star in a film about Jean-Jacques Dessalines

clues.clue: Early career: staged commedia dell'arte-inspired play Enough Stupidity in Every Wise Man
examples: Early in his career, this theorist drew on commedia dell'arte tropes to stage the play Enough Stupidity in Every Wise Man

comprehensive_summary:
Sergei Eisenstein is a Soviet film director and theorist, the central figure of Soviet montage cinema. He is most identified by the 'Odessa Steps' sequence in Battleship Potemkin (1925), in which a baby carriage rolls down steps while soldiers fire on crowds — a canonical example of his montage theory.

Eisenstein's first film was the short Glumov's Diary. His debut feature, Strike (1925), climaxes with cows being slaughtered intercut with soldiers massacring workers in a muddy field. Battleship Potemkin (1925) followed, containing the iconic Odessa Steps sequence. October (1928), about the 1917 Russian Revolution, ended with a ruler's bearded head towering over a parade — but was criticized by Soviet leaders as 'formalist.' The film inspired the name of the arts journal October, founded by Rosalind Krauss and Annette Michelson.

Alexander Nevsky (1938) restored Eisenstein's political standing with a propaganda film dramatizing the Battle of the Ice against the Teutonic Knights; a bishop wears a hat with a swastika. His planned trilogy Ivan the Terrible ended with Part II being condemned by the Soviet Central Committee as 'anti-historical'; Eisenstein died of a heart attack in 1948 after refusing to revise it. He also abandoned ¡Que Viva México! (1932), which Upton Sinclair financed at Charlie Chaplin's request, after shooting 50 hours of footage.

As a theorist, Eisenstein developed five types of montage — metric, rhythmic, tonal, overtonal, and intellectual — in collaboration with peers like Vsevolod Pudovkin. His essay collection Film Form argues 'montage is conflict' and analyzes the emotions induced by haiku and kabuki in a piece on cinema's 'fourth dimension.' Another essay argues Charles Dickens was D.W. Griffith's strongest influence. Early in his career he staged Enough Stupidity in Every Wise Man, drawing on commedia dell'arte. In a lecture he insisted Paul Robeson should star in a film about Haitian revolutionary Jean-Jacques Dessalines.
